title: Optimizing the Answer, Hiding the Reason
subtitle: Outcome-only reinforcement learning erodes chain-of-thought monitorability against a known ground-truth cue
status: Smoke complete; real run designed, awaiting launch
summary: Chain-of-thought monitoring is one of the few interpretability tools that scales with capability, but it only works if a model's stated reasoning reflects the computation that produced its answer.

references.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
references.annote: Origin of CoT prompting. The capability whose faithfulness this project interrogates.

references.title: Measuring Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
references.annote: Defines faithfulness via interventions on the CoT (truncation, mistakes, paraphrase); larger models often less faithful. The measurement framing our reliance/articulation metrics build on.

references.title: Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think: Unfaithful Explanations in Chain-of-Thought Prompting
references.annote: Foundational unfaithful-CoT result. Source of the biasing-feature paradigm (a suggested answer the model follows without admitting it) that we adapt as the planted cue. Static prompted setting, no optimization pressure.

references.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
references.annote: Introduces GRPO, the outcome-reward RL algorithm we train with.

references.title: Monitoring Reasoning Models for Misbehavior and the Risks of Promoting Obfuscation
references.annote: Direct optimization pressure on the CoT produces obfuscated reward hacking; argues for a monitorability tax. Closest mechanism precedent. Contrast: our reward is outcome-only with no direct CoT pressure, so any decay we observe is the weaker, more worrying emergent case.

references.title: Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think
references.annote: Closest prior result. Outcome-based RL improves faithfulness then plateaus; reward-hacked hint usage is not verbalized. We must position against this directly: our contribution is a controlled decay curve vs RL steps against a known ground-truth cue with provably CoT-blind reward.

references.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
references.annote: Pure-RL incentivization of reasoning. Motivates studying what outcome-only RL does to the resulting chains of thought.

references.title: Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity for AI Safety
references.annote: Multi-org position paper warning that scaled-up RL specifically threatens CoT monitorability. This project is a controlled empirical test of its central worry.

title: Error Awareness Is Format-Specific: An Arithmetic-Trained Wrongness Probe Does Not Transfer to Capital-City Facts
subtitle: A token-distribution transfer test on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
status: Real run complete (RTX 5090). In-format AUC 0.968, cross-format AUC 0.649, capitals-internal AUC 0.990. Verdict: largely format-specific.
summary: A language model often assigns a different next-token distribution to a statement it has just completed depending on whether that statement is true or false, and a small classifier reading that distribution can recover whether the statement was correct. We ask a narrower question than prior work on whether such a signal exists.

references.title: Discovering Latent Knowledge in Language Models Without Supervision
references.annote: CCS recovers a truth direction from contrastive activations without labels. Motivates the question of whether the truth/error representation is shared or domain-specific, which our transfer test probes behaviorally.

references.title: Language Models (Mostly) Know What They Know
references.annote: Models can self-evaluate the probability their answers are correct (P(IK)), with calibration that varies by task. Frames the self-knowledge our probe reads out, and its task-dependence anticipates our format-specificity result.

references.title: The Internal State of an LLM Knows When It's Lying
references.annote: Shows a model's hidden activations linearly encode whether its own statement is true or false. The clearest precedent that an internal error signal exists; we ask whether a readout of that signal transports across statement formats.

references.title: The Geometry of Truth: Emergent Linear Structure in Large Language Model Representations of True/False Datasets
references.annote: Finds linear true/false structure but reports that truth directions generalize unevenly across datasets/topics. Directly relevant: our finding that an arithmetic-trained readout barely transports to capitals is a behavioral echo of their representational topic-specificity.
